text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 55-year-old female with known Stage IV metastatic cutaneous melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant. Currently on dabrafenib plus trametinib combination targeted therapy, initiated approximately 8 weeks prior. Restaging examination to evaluate treatment response. Prior imaging includes whole-body PET-CT at diagnosis demonstrating hypermetabolic bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, multiple subcutaneous nodules of the right flank and left axillary region, and four FDG-avid hepatic lesions.

TECHNIQUE: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the uneventful intravenous administration of 100 mL of iohexol contrast material. Acquisitions were obtained in the portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis and in the standard chest protocol. Multiplanar reconstructions were generated. MRI of the brain was performed on a 1.5T scanner with standard axial T1, T2, T2 FLAIR, DWI/ADC, susceptibility-weighted imaging, and post-gadolinium T1 sequences.

COMPARISON: Whole-body 18F-FDG PET-CT performed at initial staging workup.

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK (MRI BRAIN):

The cerebral and cerebellar parenchyma are unremarkable in signal characteristics. The cortical ribbon appears intact without focal signal abnormality on T2/FLAIR sequences. No areas of restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarct. No abnormal foci of susceptibility artifact to suggest microhemorrhage or calcification. Following gadolinium administration, there are no enhancing lesions within the brain parenchyma, the leptomeninges, or along the dural surfaces. The brainstem and posterior fossa structures appear normal. Ventricles are of normal caliber and configuration. No mass effect or midline shift. The visualized calvarium is intact. The pituitary gland is unremarkable.

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The airways are patent to the segmental level. The lung parenchyma demonstrates no focal consolidation, mass, or suspicious nodule. There are no pleural effusions. The lung bases are clear. The previously noted PET-CT findings did not include pulmonary lesions, and none are identified on the current examination.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinal and hilar contours are within normal limits. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes are identified. The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. Aorta and great vessels are unremarkable.

Axillary Regions: There has been a striking and highly favorable interval change in the bilateral axillary lymph nodes compared to the staging PET-CT. On the right, the previously prominent hypermetabolic axillary lymph node, which measured approximately 2.1 cm in short axis at the time of staging, has dramatically decreased in size and now measures approximately 0.7 cm in short axis. This node, while still visible, has become much less conspicuous, with loss of the rounded morphology and restoration of a more oblate, fatty-hilum-containing configuration. On the left, the previously enlarged left axillary lymph node measuring 1.8 cm has similarly diminished markedly, now measuring approximately 0.5 cm. These findings are consistent with a robust treatment response within the axillary nodal stations bilaterally.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues: No suspicious chest wall lesion or new soft tissue mass.

Bony Thorax: No destructive osseous lesion identified in the ribs, sternum, or thoracic spine.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Liver: There has been a marked and impressive interval decrease in the hepatic tumor burden since the staging examination. At initial staging, four FDG-avid hepatic lesions were identified, with the dominant lesion in the right lobe measuring 3.1 cm. On the current examination:

- Right hepatic lobe, dominant lesion: The previously 3.1 cm hypervascular lesion has dramatically decreased in size, now measuring approximately 1.2 cm. The lesion retains some residual peripheral enhancement but is markedly smaller, with a more hypodense, potentially partially necrotic center on portal venous phase imaging.

- Right hepatic lobe, second lesion: The previously 1.8 cm lesion has substantially regressed and now measures approximately 0.6 cm. It appears as a subtle hypodense focus with minimal enhancement.

- Left hepatic lobe lesions: The two previously described left lobe lesions, measuring 1.2 cm and 0.9 cm at staging, have undergone near-complete regression. The 1.2 cm lesion is now represented by a tiny sub-centimeter focus that approaches the limit of CT resolution, and the 0.9 cm lesion is either no longer definitively identified or has involuted to below the level of confident detection. No discrete enhancing focus is identified in this region on delayed sequences.

Overall, the aggregate hepatic tumor burden has substantially diminished, representing a profound treatment response across all hepatic metastatic sites. No new hepatic lesions are identified. The liver parenchyma otherwise appears homogeneous with no biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein is patent.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesion.

Pancreas: Unremarkable. No ductal dilatation or parenchymal lesion.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are within normal limits. No adrenal mass or nodule.

Kidneys and Ureters: Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and demonstrate symmetric and symmetric contrast enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal mass. Bilateral renal cysts not present.

Bowel: Small and large bowel loops are unremarkable in caliber and wall thickness. No evidence of obstruction or inflammatory change. Appendix is normal.

Pelvis: Uterus and ovaries are within normal limits for age. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No free pelvic fluid.

Retroperitoneum: No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. No retroperitoneal mass.

SUBCUTANEOUS SOFT TISSUES:

Right Flank: The right flank subcutaneous nodule, which measured up to 1.4 cm in the largest dimension on the staging PET-CT and was clinically palpable at consultation, has regressed profoundly. On the current examination it measures approximately 0.3 cm and is barely perceptible as a tiny subcutaneous soft tissue density. It is now below the threshold of reliable clinical palpation. No other soft tissue nodules of the right flank are identified.

Left Axillary Subcutaneous Region: The previously noted subcutaneous nodules in the left axillary region have substantially involuted and are not reliably identified as discrete lesions on the current CT.

Remainder of evaluated soft tissues, including the upper back and posterior shoulder at the site of the excised primary lesion (with expected postsurgical changes), are unremarkable. No new subcutaneous nodule or suspicious soft tissue density is identified elsewhere.

BONE WINDOWS:

No lytic or blastic osseous lesion identified in the imaged skeleton. Vertebral body heights are maintained throughout the thoracic and lumbar spine.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Markedly favorable interval change in all sites of known metastatic melanoma following approximately 8 weeks of dabrafenib/trametinib targeted therapy. The overall disease burden is dramatically reduced compared to the baseline staging PET-CT.

2. Bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy has regressed substantially, with the right axillary node decreasing from 2.1 cm to 0.7 cm and the left axillary node from 1.8 cm to 0.5 cm. Both nodes are substantially smaller and approaching a more normal morphology.

3. Hepatic metastatic burden has profoundly decreased across all four previously identified lesions. The dominant right lobe lesion has reduced from 3.1 cm to 1.2 cm. The second right lobe lesion has reduced from 1.8 cm to 0.6 cm. The two left lobe lesions have regressed to near or below the level of CT detection.

4. The previously identified and palpable right flank subcutaneous nodule has regressed from 1.4 cm to approximately 0.3 cm, approaching resolution.

5. Previously noted left axillary subcutaneous nodules are not reliably identified, consistent with complete or near-complete regression at this site.

6. No new sites of metastatic disease are identified.

7. MRI brain: No evidence of intracranial metastases. No leptomeningeal disease.

These findings are consistent with a substantial and multifocal treatment response across all previously identified sites of metastatic disease. No new organ involvement is demonstrated.

Attending Radiologist: [Electronically signed]
Resident: [Electronically signed]
